name: Reserve Bank of India
summary: Day 68 Wednesday. **RBI CAPITAL-FLOW DEFENSIVE MEASURES FORMALIZED — DEFENDED INR THROUGH MAY 4-6 STRESS WITHOUT DIRECT INTERVENTION.** Two specific operational tools: (1) capped Net Open Position in INR of dealer banks at $100mn/day; (2) barred domestic banks from offering INR NDF (non-deliverable forward) contracts to non-resident Indians (NRI) and related parties (CONFIRMED — RBI primary directives, Trading Pedia Apr 30 reference, Bloomberg Apr 10 framework). These curbs explain why INR pulled back below ₹95 on May 6 despite the May 4-5 record-low intraday print. RBI policy rate held at 5.25%; FX reserves >$700 billion (~11 months import cover). Governor Malhotra's 'FX curbs won't remain forever' framing remains operative. The ₹95.50 direct-intervention threshold was NOT tested through the kinetic-restart stress — the curbs alone proved sufficient. Important nuance: NOP/NDF curbs are defensive containment, NOT a sustainable equilibrium — if Brent breaks $115 again or FII outflows accelerate beyond ₹2.5 lakh crore CY26, ₹95.50 still in play. India 10Y GOI ~6.90% (-10 bps area, steady). FII outflow context: CY26 cumulative ₹1.92 lakh crore = $23B+; DII (SIP-driven) absorbed ~90%. The RBI's documented preference is volatility management rather than defending a specific level — confirmed through this cycle.
